serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tonkatsu omelette curry is approximately 500 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice, a breaded deep-fried pork cutlet, a soft omelette, and Japanese curry sauce ladled over the plate.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — pork loin breaded fried (NDB 10219), egg omelette (NDB 01132), Japanese curry sauce with vegetables (adapted from USDA 'beef curry with sauce'), and cooked white rice (NDB 20444). Weighted composite per standard serving proportions.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for tonkatsu omelette curry as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries for breaded fried pork cutlet, egg omelette, Japanese-style curry sauce with vegetables, and cooked white rice, combined in proportions representative of a standard 500 g restaurant serving (approximately 200 g rice, 120 g breaded pork cutlet, 80 g omelette, 100 g curry sauce with vegetables). Per-100 g values are derived from the weighted composite.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed rice-based Japanese meals estimated from analogous composite: white rice GI 64 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr) weighted with low-GI protein and fat components (breaded pork, egg, curry sauce) reducing composite GI to approximately 54. Composite GI estimation methodology per Wolever & Jenkins (1986).
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tonkatsu omelette curry. White rice GI of 64 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) is the primary glycemic driver. The substantial protein (pork cutlet, egg) and fat content of the dish attenuate the glycemic response. Composite GI estimated at 54 using the Wolever-Jenkins weighted-component method. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 102.5) / 100 ≈ 55.4, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 20.5 × 5 = 102.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry roux and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g serving provides approximately 1,900 mg sodium, representing 83–95% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt curry roux, and avoid adding soy sauce or extra seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load from white rice despite moderate composite GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the composite GI is estimated at 54 (low category), the large carbohydrate load per serving (~102 g) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 55; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting cauliflower rice, or splitting the meal to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from deep-fried pork cutlet and curry roux
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying of the pork cutlet contributes saturated fat (~9.5 g per serving) and the curry roux adds significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, choose leaner cuts, and request lower-sodium curry preparations to reduce atherogenic and hypertensive risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tonkatsu Omelette Curry (とんかつオムレツカレー, Tonkatsu Omuretsu Karē) is a Japanese comfort-food dish that combines three iconic elements of Japanese cuisine: tonkatsu (a deep-fried breaded pork cutlet), a soft pan-fried omelette, and Japanese curry sauce, all served over a bed of steamed white rice. The pork cutlet is coated in panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then placed atop the rice alongside or beneath a lightly set omelette. A rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce — typically made from a commercial curry roux block containing onion, carrot, and potato — is ladled generously over the plate. The result is a hearty, satisfying meal with contrasting textures: the crunch of the breaded cutlet, the silky softness of the egg, and the thick, umami-rich curry sauce binding the components together. Japanese curry is notably milder and sweeter than South Asian curries, with a thick, stew-like consistency. This dish is popular in Japanese curry restaurants and yoshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) establishments across Japan and in Japanese restaurant chains in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g serving is energy-dense (~875 kcal), high in carbohydrates from white rice, moderate in protein from pork and egg, and notable for its sodium content from the curry roux. The composite glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low category), though the glycemic load per serving is high due to the large rice portion. Clinically, the dish warrants attention for sodium content in hypertensive individuals and carbohydrate load management in those with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for tonkatsu omelette curry as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries for breaded fried pork cutlet, egg omelette, Japanese-style curry sauce, and cooked white rice. GI is estimated using the Wolever-Jenkins composite method anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database white rice value. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and documented, but direct measurement data for this specific dish combination is not available in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as tonkatsu omelette curry is a composite rice-based curry dish with pork, egg, and vegetables — not a dairy or egg product. 2. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy; no HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this dish. 3. GI estimated at 54 (low) using composite method; white rice GI anchor from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). 4. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for estimation. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting minor trans fat contribution from deep-frying oil; this is a plausible estimated value, not confirmed zero. 6. Halal status is false because pork is a primary ingredient. 7. Sodium content is high due to commercial curry roux; restaurant-specific sodium may vary. 8. The trailing period in the input food_name 'Tonkatsu Omelette Curry.' has been removed to produce the canonical English name.
